schools.summary: Nahash the Ammonite invades Gilead to renew Jephthah's old quarrel; the siege likely preceded Saul's election (1 Samuel 12:12), the LXX adding 'about a month.'
Matthew Henry: the men of Jabesh, having lost the virtue of Israelites, lost their valour too, and stooped to serve an Ammonite without a struggle.
Gill traces the Ammonite grudge to Jephthah's day and marks Jabesh's exposed border position.
Wesley: 'Then' means 'about that time'; the invasion may have prompted, or merely coincided with, the choice of a king.

debates.question: Did Nahash's invasion come before or after Saul's election as king?

sides.position: before — Israel sought a king because they saw Nahash coming (1 Samuel 12:12)
his preparation prompted the demand, but his actual coming may have waited until the king was chosen

schools.summary: The right-eye mutilation both disables for war (the left eye being hidden by the shield) and insults all Israel; Nahash avenges Jephthah's old defeat.
Clarke cites Theodoret and Josephus on the military logic; Wesley: partly for reproach, partly to disable, leaving one eye for menial service.
Gill hears the reply given in a haughty and scornful manner.

themes: the right eye and the shield

schools.summary: Nahash grants the delay expecting no help; sending to 'all the coasts' shows Israel had no central government and had not heard of Saul's election. Ellicott recalls the ancient Benjamin-Jabesh bond of Judges 21.
Gill notes it was the magistrates and principal men of the city who made the request.
The messengers came first to Gibeah — Saul's residence and Benjamin's seat; Saul, ignorant, learns the news only on returning from the field, and the people can only weep.
Gill: 'Gibeah of Saul,' so named now in his honour; Benjamin owed Jabesh a debt, having taken wives thence when the tribe was nearly extinct (Judges 21).
Clarke: the appeal was made to the people, not to Saul; they saw no hope and feared the reproach would fall on all Israel.
Matthew Henry: Saul's humility, neighbourliness, and zeal — unpuffed by advancement, he works with his hands and grieves with his people.
Ellicott: the monarchy was new and vague, so amid jealousies Saul kept the royalty in the background; a hero summoned from the plough like Gideon and Cincinnatus.
Wesley: only anointed, not inaugurated, Saul forbore royal state; Clarke notes agriculture was the ancient calling of rulers, citing Ovid.
Gill: Saul returned to his father's house and its rustic employments.
Gill: the Spirit filled Saul with compassion, wisdom, and fortitude (Targum, 'the spirit of fortitude'); his anger burned against Nahash's insult to Israel.
The Spirit falls on Saul as on Othniel and the judges (Judges 3:10), enduing him with valour; his wrath is kindled at the shame designed against all Israel.
Clarke: the 'kindled anger' means his courage was greatly excited, strong for fight and confident of success.
The dismembered oxen echo Judges 19:29; Saul joins Samuel's name, still honouring the prophet; the 'fear of Jehovah' is real awe, not panic — the same Spirit lifting the whole people (Psalms 78:65).
Wesley: the sight affects the heart more than hearing; Samuel's name lends authority; the God-sent fear made none dare withhold help — the fear of God makes good subjects and soldiers.
Gill: the oxen were Saul's own or his father's, just followed out of the field.
Bezek located north of Nablus; the numbers fit a general levy; the separate reckoning of Judah already hints at the tribe's habitual standing-apart until it had a king of its own.

Wesley: Judah's separate mention is to its honour, readily submitting to a king of another tribe though the kingdom was promised to it (Genesis 49:10). Clarke doubts the numbers, suspecting scribal exaggeration.
Gill records Bezek as the rendezvous and the old alternate reading of the word as a 'stone' or 'fragment' used in counting.

debates.question: Why is Judah numbered apart, and what does its small figure mean?

sides.position: a trace of Judah's habitual separation from Israel, foreshadowing the later schism
to Judah's honour — willing loyalty to a king of another tribe though the promise was hers

schools.summary: 'When the sun be hot' means about noon — relief well within Nahash's seven days; Bezek to Jabesh was not much over twenty miles, so the promise was credible.
Gill takes the plural 'they said' of Saul and Samuel answering the men of Jabesh with one voice.
The message is a stratagem — a feigned surrender meant to lull Nahash so the besiegers may be surprised by Saul's advancing army and thoroughly beaten.
Clarke: they concealed the news of Saul's help; they did come out, 'but in a different manner' than the Ammonites expected.
Three companies strike the camp from three sides in the morning watch (as Gideon in Judges 7:16); Saul marched by night over Jordan, and the rout was complete — not two left together.
Matthew Henry: Saul was better than his word, coming before daybreak — 'whom God helps, he helps right early' (Psalms 46:5) — and surrounded the dreaming foe on every side.
Gill fixes the timing 'on the morrow,' after the men of Jabesh had lulled the besiegers.
The demand recalls the worthless men who despised Saul at Mizpeh (1 Samuel 10:27); the appeal to Samuel shows his enduring weight even in the flush of Saul's victory.
Matthew Henry: the people's jealousy for Saul's honour is mixed with the baser truth that his success, not his anointing, now confirmed his title in their eyes.
Clarke: flushed with victory they would slay their opponents in cool blood — 'he has shed blood, he wishes to shed more.'
Gill wonders how the aged Samuel kept pace, and supposes he met the army soon after the battle.
A wise and generous decision that averted future feuds and showed humble piety; since God had shown clemency in the victory, Saul returns clemency (Seb. Schmidt, in Keil).
Clarke: the pardon was as much to Saul's credit as the proposed slaughter was to the soldiers' discredit.
Gill: lenity and prudence at the reign's outset — though this gracious temper did not always continue with Saul.
Matthew Henry: power is for edification, not destruction; the once-despised king who pardons rebels foreshadows the once-despised Saviour who now receives the submission of rebels.

themes: no blood on a day of salvation

schools.summary: Ellicott: Gilgal, the ancient sanctuary near Jericho; Samuel generously re-presents Saul, whom the Mizpeh election had left with many murmurings against him.
Wesley praises Samuel's prudence in choosing the fittest season to confirm the choice; Clarke notes the first unction was private, and reports Josephus that Saul was anointed a second time.
Gill: Samuel spoke agreeing with Saul, partly to draw the people off their demand for the offenders' lives while their temper was gracious.
Not a fresh anointing but a solemn national confirmation of the Mizpeh election; 'before the Lord' implies the Ark or the high priest with Urim; the people make Saul assume royal state (Wordsworth).
Clarke thinks a second anointing likely — Saul now publicly acknowledged with no gainsayer — yet notes he soon lost the kingdom through imprudence; Wesley: 'made' = owned and accepted him as king.
Gill: all the people agreed and marched with Saul and Samuel to Gilgal.

debates.question: Was Saul anointed a second time at Gilgal?

sides.position: yes — likely a second anointing, as the LXX and Josephus indicate
no — only a solemn confirmation; a fresh unction would have been recorded
